quote: we were supposed to have a phone call with you, actually, yesterday to show you that solar estimate, but we weren't able to reach you. And so we're just looking to get you
stewart_read: Holland opens a reschedule call by explaining the missed connection — a logistics frame — rather than re-establishing legitimacy and the customer's original motivation. On a recycled/reschedule touch, the intro beat still applies: the customer's interest has cooled since the original inquiry, and the first 30 seconds are the window to re-warm it. Holland skips this entirely. Strategic read: Holland may be deliberately keeping it brief to avoid re-opening objections on a cooperative customer. Reactive read: Holland defaulted to logistics because there was no script for reschedule re-engagement. The reactive read is more supported — there is no evidence of a deliberate strategy to re-anchor later in the call.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland kept the opener brief deliberately to avoid re-opening objections on a customer who was already cooperative and calling back voluntarily.
coaching_implication: For reschedule calls, the manager should work with Holland on a one-sentence re-anchor before the scheduling ask — something that surfaces the customer's original motivation without re-pitching. The schema intro section covers legitimacy establishment but does not yet have a reschedule variant; this is a gap worth flagging for Kenny.

quote: we were just looking to to see if we can get you back on this schedule just because the specialist has some calls going on today. So I don't think you would have time today, but we could totally schedule for tomorrow if you have some time.
stewart_read: This is the natural moment to briefly surface bill pain before asking for the new slot — 'before we find a time, I just want to make sure we're still set up to show you what your bill could look like' — but Holland goes straight to availability. The bill anchor is never touched in this call. Per the schema, bill capture and emotional flip are required work; on a reschedule call where the bill may already be on file, the flip is the remaining job. Holland does neither.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have assumed the bill was already captured from the prior touch and that re-raising it would feel redundant to the customer.
coaching_implication: Manager should ask Holland: 'What did you know about this customer's bill before the call?' If the bill was on file, the coaching is about the emotional flip — using the known number to re-anchor motivation before re-booking. If the bill was not on file, the coaching is about why it wasn't collected on this call. Either way, the bill anchor is the missing piece.

quote: Or I could do, like, a lunchtime sometime.
stewart_read: This is the single most active moment of customer engagement in the call. The customer volunteers a scheduling preference unprompted — they are working to make the appointment happen. This is a small but real signal that the customer still has some residual interest. Holland uses it only for scheduling logistics. The coaching opportunity: when a customer leans in to make an appointment work, that is the crack to briefly surface their original motivation — 'I love that you want to make this work — just so we're set up well for Thursday, can I ask what was it about your bill that got you interested in the first place?' One sentence. Then confirm the slot.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have read the customer's scheduling flexibility as sufficient engagement signal and prioritized a clean, frictionless re-book over any risk of re-opening the conversation.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this moment to show Holland what a re-anchor looks like in a reschedule context. The customer's initiative here was a gift — it opened a door Holland didn't walk through. Tie to coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring: the flip doesn't require a long conversation, just a single question before confirming the slot.

quote: Yeah. So Thursday at 01:30, and then it will just be a phone call. So if you could just have, like, access to your email during the call, just that way he can email you over all the info.
stewart_read: Holland's button-up is incomplete. The email-access ask is present, but the required elements are missing: no computer anchor ('you'll be able to sit down to review the design on your computer'), no no-driving requirement, and no 'is that fair' verbal handshake. Per the schema, the handshake is predictive of show rate and should not be skipped. The email mention also introduces a potential expectation gap — the customer may expect to receive the proposal by email before the call, which conflicts with the hard rule in rebuttals.send_proposal_before_meeting.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have intentionally kept the button-up light to avoid over-complicating a cooperative close on a short reschedule call.
coaching_implication: Manager should walk Holland through the full button_up checklist and ask which elements were intentionally skipped vs forgotten. The 'is that fair' handshake is the highest-priority missing piece — it is the verbal commitment that anchors show rate. The email mention should also be reviewed: if the customer interprets this as 'you'll email me the proposal,' the closer walks into a protocol conflict.

quote: I I someone called in to this number and actually hung up.
stewart_read: The customer opens by explaining a confusing prior interaction — someone called their number and hung up. This is a minor friction signal: the customer's first experience of this re-engagement was a hang-up, which could seed low-grade skepticism about the professionalism of the outreach. Holland acknowledges it briefly ('Yeah. So it looks like we...') and moves on. The handling is adequate for the moment, but the schema does not yet have a reschedule-specific intro protocol. This is a gap worth noting — on a recycled lead where the prior touch was a missed call, the first 15 seconds carry extra legitimacy weight.
alternative_hypothesis: The hang-up was likely a dialer drop, not a meaningful trust event, and Holland's quick acknowledgment was sufficient.
coaching_implication: Low priority for this call, but worth flagging for the schema: reschedule calls where the prior touch was a missed or dropped call benefit from a brief legitimacy re-establishment ('that was our system — sorry about that, here's who I am and why I'm calling back'). Not a Holland-specific gap so much as a floor-wide script gap for Kenny to address.

quote: Perfect. I can do that. Alrighty. Well, thank you so much, and we will talk to you on Thursday.
stewart_read: Holland closes the call without sending or promising a personalized text follow-up. Per button_up.text_followup_promise, the setter must inform the customer that a text with appointment details will follow. The text_after_call template requires a tieback to the customer's interests or concerns — which Holland cannot write because no motivation was surfaced on this call. The missing text promise is both a protocol gap and a downstream no-show risk: the customer has no written confirmation of Thursday 1:30.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may send the text after the call without mentioning it on the call — but the schema requires the promise to be made during the call, not just the action taken.
coaching_implication: Manager should confirm whether Holland sent a follow-up text after this call. If yes, the gap is the on-call promise only. If no, the gap is both the promise and the action. Either way, the tieback problem remains: the text template requires a reference to something the customer said, and Holland has nothing to tie back to. This is a downstream consequence of the missing motivation re-anchor.
